# UC-14.1.5 · Elevator/Equipment Health

## Description

Equipment fault codes enable predictive maintenance, reducing downtime and extending equipment life.

## Value

Equipment fault codes enable predictive maintenance, reducing downtime and extending equipment life.

## Implementation

Forward BMS fault events to Splunk. Map fault codes to descriptions via lookup. Track fault frequency per equipment. Alert on critical faults. Report on recurring issues for maintenance planning.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: BMS integration, MQTT.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: BMS event logs, equipment fault codes.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Forward BMS fault events to Splunk. Map fault codes to descriptions via lookup. Track fault frequency per equipment. Alert on critical faults. Report on recurring issues for maintenance planning.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=bms sourcetype="bms:faults"
| stats count by equipment_id, fault_code, description
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Elevator/Equipment Health** — Equipment fault codes enable predictive maintenance, reducing downtime and extending equipment life.

Documented **Data sources**: BMS event logs, equipment fault codes. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): BMS integration, MQTT. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: bms; **sourcetype**: bms:faults. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=bms, sourcetype="bms:faults". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by equipment_id, fault_code, description** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Run the search in a known window. Spot-check a few events against the BMS console (for example Niagara, Tridium, or Honeywell) and confirm zones, values, and alarms line up. Verify field names and that the right roles can search the index.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (equipment faults), Bar chart (faults by equipment), Timeline (fault events).

## SPL

```spl
index=bms sourcetype="bms:faults"
| stats count by equipment_id, fault_code, description
| sort -count
```

## Visualization

Table (equipment faults), Bar chart (faults by equipment), Timeline (fault events).
